planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil and allow to cold-stratify over winter; alternatively plant container-grown vines in spring near a light trellis, shrub, or fence in partial sun with consistently moist to wet soil.

directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial sun along woodland edges, swamp margins, stream banks, or moist fence lines; swamp leatherflower is one of the most beautiful native vines of the Gulf Coast and southeastern US, producing delicate, nodding, urn-shaped flowers with flared, crisped petal tips in shades of lavender-blue to pale violet throughout summer. Unlike many clematis it thrives in poorly drained and seasonally flooded soils, making it invaluable for wet spots where other vines fail. It climbs by twining leaf petioles to 3–6 feet through shrubs, other vines, or light structures and pairs naturally with buttonbush, swamp rose, and Virginia willow in wet garden borders. The feathery seed heads are ornamental in fall. This species is best grown from nursery-propagated plants; purchase from reputable native plant nurseries as wild collection is discouraged.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed immediately after harvest in late spring or early summer directly into the garden or into outdoor containers; seed requires a warm period followed by cold stratification and will not germinate until the following spring. Alternatively, plant nursery-grown divisions in fall in neutral to slightly alkaline, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full shade.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil that is neutral to slightly alkaline (pH 6.5–7.5) in full to partial shade, ideally under deciduous trees where early spring sunlight reaches the forest floor before leaf-out; sharp-lobed hepatica is among the earliest wildflowers of the eastern North American woodland, pushing up delicate white, pink, or lavender flowers on fuzzy stems in February and March before any leaves are visible, often while snow still lingers. It is distinguished from the closely related round-lobed hepatica (H. americana) by its three-pointed, sharply tipped leaf lobes and its preference for neutral to calcareous rather than acidic soils, making it the better choice for limestone-derived soils in the Midwest and mid-Atlantic. The mottled, three-lobed evergreen leaves persist through winter and are attractive throughout the season. Plant in drifts of 10 or more for best effect; it spreads slowly by self-sowing in ideal conditions and is long-lived once established. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall, pressing lightly into the soil surface without covering; seed requires cold stratification over winter and germinates in early spring. Established plants can also be divided in early spring before significant growth resumes.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, average-fertility soil in partial shade to full sun; skunk meadow rue is distinguished from the closely related purple meadow rue (Thalictrum dasycarpum) by its waxy, glaucous, gland-dotted leaflets with slightly revolute (rolled-under) margins and its musky, skunk-like scent when the foliage is bruised — a distinctive odor that gives the plant its common name. It tolerates drier sites than most meadow rues and performs well in open woodland margins, prairie edges, and sunny borders with average soil. The airy, lavender to white flowers are borne in branched panicles from June through July and are highly attractive to a wide range of native bees, wasps, and beneficial insects. The blue-green, fernlike foliage provides exceptional textural contrast in the mixed border all season. A graceful, underused native that combines beautifully with wild bergamot, rattlesnake master, and prairie dropseed.

planting.note: Scarify or soak seed overnight before direct sowing in spring into any well-drained, sunny site; inoculate with appropriate Rhizobium inoculant for best nitrogen fixation. WARNING: Crown vetch is listed as invasive in numerous US states and should not be planted near natural areas, woodland edges, or native plant communities.

directions: Direct sow scarified seed in spring in full sun in any well-drained soil — crown vetch is exceptionally adaptable to poor, dry, rocky, or compacted soils and requires virtually no maintenance once established; it was widely planted for highway slope stabilization and erosion control throughout the 20th century and is extremely effective at holding disturbed soil. The dense, spreading mats of pinnate foliage bear clusters of small pink-and-white pea flowers throughout summer that are attractive to bees. INVASIVE WARNING: Crown vetch spreads aggressively by both rhizome and seed and is now listed as invasive or a noxious weed in many US states, where it smothers native vegetation in woodland margins, old fields, and natural areas. Do not plant within a quarter mile of natural areas, forest edges, or native plant communities. Consider native alternatives for erosion control such as Virginia wild rye (Elymus virginicus), wild ginger (Asarum canadense), or prairie dropseed (Sporobolus heterolepis).

planting.note: Sow fresh seed immediately after harvest in late spring directly into the garden; seed requires warm-then-cold double dormancy and will not germinate until the second spring after a double stratification period. Alternatively sow in fall outdoors and allow to stratify naturally over winter for spring germination.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full to partial shade under deciduous trees or in a shaded rock garden; false rue anemone is a charming and delicate small European woodland perennial in the buttercup family, closely related to the North American native Enemion (with which it was once synonymous), producing a profusion of small five-petaled white flowers on thread-fine stems from March through May before going fully dormant by early summer. The lacy, blue-green, thrice-divided leaves closely resemble those of meadow rue (Thalictrum) and rue anemone (Thalictrum thalictroides), making it a beautiful companion for both in the spring woodland garden. It spreads slowly by self-sowing to form loose, delicate colonies and is especially effective planted in drifts in shaded rock garden crevices, at the base of stone walls, or along woodland paths. A refined and uncommon plant that rewards the collector of shade perennials with its elegant early-spring display.
